coredata.dc:title: A carbon dot-based fluorescence method for selective quantification of sulfide in environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Abi, Alireza
Kazemi, Ghasem
Safavi, Afsaneh
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid growth of agricultural and industrial sectors, a significant quantity of sulfide is released into water resources systems at an increasing rate. This has caused serious environmental problems, which calls for the development of sensitive and selective methods for determining sulfide in environmental samples. In this report, we employed carbon dots (C-dots) as fluorescence emitters and measured the decrease in their fluorescence intensity caused by Ag2S formation. Instead of having free Ag+ cation (which can interact with many other species besides sulfide) in the C-dot solution, we used iodide (I−) to convert Ag+ to one of the least soluble silver salts (i.e., AgI). While AgI interacts with sulfide to form Ag2S (because of the lower solubility of Ag2S), it remains stable against many other anions, thereby offering the method high selectivity. Under optimized experimental conditions, the developed method enabled quantifying sulfide in the range of 1–10 μM with the low detection limit of 0.48 μM. The applicability of the method was demonstrated by successful analysis of sulfide in a mineral water, a hot spring water, and an industrial wastewater sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Fluorescence method
Sulfide detection
Silver iodide
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Real sample analysis using the C-dot/AgI assay
Characterization of the citric acid-derived C-dots
C-dots/AgI as a selective fluorescent probe for sulfide analysis
Effect of pH and AgI concentration
Determination of sulfide using the C-dot/AgI fluorescent probe
Determination of sulfide in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) FTIR spectrum, (C) XRD pattern, and (D) UV–vis absorption and normalized emission spectra of the citric acid-derived C-dots. Inset in panel D is a digital photo of the C-dots taken with UV light.

(A) The fluorescence intensity of the solution containing 30 μg mL−1 C-dot and 200 μM Ag+ before (a) and after adding 50 μM of I− (b), SCN− (c), GSH (d), or S2− (e) to the solution. (B) The fluorescence intensity of the solution containing 30 μg mL−1 C-dot, 200 μM AgI, and 100 mM NaI before (a) and after adding 50 μM of I− (b), SCN− (c), GSH (d), or S2− (e) to the solution. Insets show (F 0-F)/F 0 for each of the added species (data represent the mean ± standard deviation (SD) of three replicate experiments). All measurements were carried out in 10 mM BR buffer (pH 12).

Normalized excitation/emission spectra of 30 μg mL−1 C-dot, and UV–vis absorption spectra of 200 μM AgI in the absence and presence of 50 μM Na2S. Spectra were recorded in 10 mM BR buffer (pH 12)/100 mM NaI.
Schematic illustration of the C-dot/AgI fluorescence method for the detection of sulfide.

(A) Plot of (F 0-F)/F 0 versus sulfide concentration for the C-dot solution containing 25 and 250 μM AgI. (B) Dependence of (F 0-F)/F 0 on AgI concentration obtained for two sulfide levels of 5 and 10 μM. Data represent the mean ± SD of three replicate experiments. Measurements were carried out in 10 mM BR buffer (pH 12)/100 mM NaI and C-dot concentration was 30 μg mL−1.

Plot of fluorescence intensity of the C-dot solution containing 50 μM AgI versus Na2S concentration. Inset shows the corresponding calibration curve. Data represent the mean ± SD of three replicate experiments. Measurements were carried out in 10 mM BR buffer (pH 12)/100 mM NaI and C-dot concentration was 30 μg mL−1.

Signal changes observed for the C-dot solution containing 50 μM AgI in the presence of various species. Measurements were carried out in the presence of each species individually (open bars) or together with 7 μM S2− (hatched bars). The concentration of added substances was 7 μM (for S2−), 70 μM (for SCN−, PO4 3−, P2O7 4−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O7 2−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, BrO3 −, NO3 −, CH3COO−, NO2 −, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, IO3 −, and cys), and 14 μM (for HCOO−, CN−, and GSH). Data represent the mean ± SD of three replicate experiments. C-dot concentration was 30 μg mL−1, and measurements were carried out in 10 mM BR buffer (pH 12)/100 mM NaI.
Comparison of some of the recently reported optical methods for determination of sulfide.

row.entry: mercapto tetrazine protected fluorescent gold nanodots

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: LR: linear range; DL: detection limit.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of sulfide in real samples using the proposed fluorescence method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Dilution factors were 0.2335, 0.0056, and 0.2335 for the mineral water, hot spring water, and industrial wastewater samples, respectively.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values correspond to the found concentrations of S2− in diluted samples and are expressed as the mean of three replicate measurements.

head.ce:title: A carbon dot-based fluorescence method for selective quantification of sulfide in environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 7194684795, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid growth of agricultural and industrial sectors, a significant quantity of sulfide is released into water resources systems at an increasing rate. This has caused serious environmental problems, which calls for the development of sensitive and selective methods for determining sulfide in environmental samples. In this report, we employed carbon dots (C-dots) as fluorescence emitters and measured the decrease in their fluorescence intensity caused by Ag2S formation. Instead of having free Ag+ cation (which can interact with many other species besides sulfide) in the C-dot solution, we used iodide (I−) to convert Ag+ to one of the least soluble silver salts (i.e., AgI). While AgI interacts with sulfide to form Ag2S (because of the lower solubility of Ag2S), it remains stable against many other anions, thereby offering the method high selectivity. Under optimized experimental conditions, the developed method enabled quantifying sulfide in the range of 1–10 μM with the low detection limit of 0.48 μM. The applicability of the method was demonstrated by successful analysis of sulfide in a mineral water, a hot spring water, and an industrial wastewater sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is a highly toxic gas, exposure to high concentration of which can cause a range of adverse health effects [1]. In nature, the gas is found in places where the breakdown of organic matter takes place under low oxygen conditions, such as volcanoes, swamps, sulfur springs, hydrocarbon deposits, and stagnant bodies of water [2]. H2S is released into the environment also through industrial activities and can be found near oil and gas processing sites, geothermal power plants, tanneries, and pulp/paper mills. In aqueous media, H2S dissociates into hydrosulfide (HS−) and sulfide (S2−) anions, and the proportions of these three forms are determined by the solution pH. Since the concentration of sulfide species in environmental waters can affect the survival, productivity, and distribution of aquatic organisms [3], the development of analytical methods for selective and sensitive determination of these compounds in water samples is of great importance.

Up to now, various analytical methods have been developed for quantifying sulfide. These include electrochemical [4–7], colorimetric [8–11], resonance light scattering [12], and fluorometric [13–19] methods, to name a few. Among them, fluorescence-based methods have the advantage of high sensitivity and selectivity, which makes them suitable for determining sulfide levels in environmental samples. A new class of luminescence emitters that has recently been employed in fluorescence methods are carbon dots (C-dots) [20]. These small carbon nanoparticles have dimensions below 10 nm and can be synthesized via bottom-up methods (e.g., supported synthetic, combustion/thermal, and microwave methods) in which C-dots are formed from molecular precursors or top-down approaches (e.g., laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical oxidation) where C-dots are prepared from larger carbon structures [20]. Similar to traditional semiconductor-based quantum dots, C-dots have the advantage of size- and wavelength-dependent luminescence emission and resistance to photobleaching over conventional organic dyes. In addition, their low cost, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, chemical inertness, and ease of synthesis have made C-dots even more attracting than semiconductor quantum dots. As a result, C-dots have been widely used in recent years for a broad range of applications, particularly for sensing purposes [21].

Several researchers have employed C-dots as fluorescent probes for sulfide analysis [22–24]. These methods often rely on the interaction of sulfide with a metal ion to form a stable product and the following change in the fluorescence intensity upon this interaction. For example, Zhu and coworkers [22] developed a two-photon fluorescence method for sulfide detection, in which C-dots were conjugated to a molecular unit with an affinity for Cu2+ to form a complex. The fluorescence of the C-dots was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ but restored with the addition of Na2S (S2− was able to release Cu2+ from the complex by forming CuS). This method enabled sulfide analysis in the range of 5–100 μM with a detection limit of 0.7 μM. Liang et al. [23] reported a ratiometric fluorescence assay for detecting sulfide down to 18 nM based on the formation of Au2S, which inhibited the Förster-resonance-energy transfer between their C-dots and gold nanoclusters. Recently, Barati et al. [24] reported on two C-dot-based fluorescent probes for the indirect determination of sulfide. They exploited the interaction of sulfide with Hg2+ and Ag+ to form HgS and Ag2S, respectively, and measured the variation in the C-dots fluorescent intensity upon these interactions. Both C-dot/Hg2+ and C-dot/Ag+ probes realized sulfide analysis with low detection limit values of 0.32 μM and 0.43 μM, respectively [24].

In spite of the good sensing sensitivity achieved by using the reaction between sulfide and metal ions for sulfide detection, the interaction of some other anions with the employed metal ions may interfere with the sulfide analysis. As an example, in the sulfide assay developed by Wang et al. [25], the presence of not only sulfide but also iodide (I−) resulted in the quenched electrochemiluminescence intensity of the C-dot/nanosilver composite. This significant interference of I− in the sulfide analysis was attributed to the formation of silver iodide (AgI), which has a small solubility product constant (K sp) of 8.52 × 10−17 [26]. Although the effect of interfering species can be minimized by using masking agents, employing methods with higher selectivity is beneficial as they prevent the complexity added to the analysis procedure.

In this work, we present a selective and sensitive C-dot-based fluorescence method for determining sulfide in aquatic media. The method is based on the interaction of AgI with sulfide to form Ag2S and the resultant quenching of the C-dots fluorescence intensity. The high selectivity of the method originates from the employment of the low soluble AgI, which, contrary to free Ag+, was unreactive to many potential interfering species.

Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), and sodium iodide (NaI) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid (anhydrous) was from Merck. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without purification. Deionized water was used throughout all the experiments, unless otherwise mentioned.

UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu spectrophotometer and a quartz cell of 1 cm path length. Fluorescence measurements were carried out using Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a Xenon flash lamp as the excitation source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a Philips CM30 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. Zeta potential was determined using a HORIBA nanoparticle analyzer (SZ-100), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer (Spectrum RXI). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained using a PANalytical X'Pert PRO MPD diffractometer; an incident beam of Cu Kα (1.54060 Å) radiation, a step size of 0.0260° 2θ, and a scan step time of 37.9950 s were employed to obtain data over the 5–80° 2θ range. Iodometric titration was conducted using a Metrohm 716 DMS Titrino.

C-dots were prepared through thermal decomposition of citric acid [27]. In a typical synthesis, 10 g of anhydrous citric acid was added to a round-bottom flask and thermally decomposed at 250 °C for 1 h while stirring at 300 rpm. The dark red product was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and the resultant solution was centrifuged at 3900 rpm for 30 min to eliminate any larger particles. The supernatant was dialyzed against 0.5 L of deionized water using a cellulose membrane. After 24 h, the dialysate was replaced with 0.5 L of fresh deionized water and the dialysis was continued for another 24 h. The obtained dark orange solution was vacuum dried at ambient temperature, and the final product was stored until needed.

Sulfide was determined using 30 μg mL−1 C-dot and 50 μM AgI in 10 mM Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 12)/0.1 M NaI. For preparing this solution, first 0.3 mL of 0.5 mM NaI was mixed with 0.4 mL of 0.225 mg mL−1 C-dot solution. Then, 0.3 mL of 0.5 mM AgNO3 was added dropwise to the C-dot/NaI solution while the mixture was stirred at 300 rpm and protected from light. The obtained solution was then mixed with 2 mL of a solution containing 15 mM BR buffer (pH 12) and 0.15 M NaI. The prepared C-dot/AgI solution was protected from light and left for at least 2 h before use in the fluorimetric determination of sulfide. An excitation wavelength of 355 nm was employed for the fluorescence measurements. When sulfide was added to the C-dot/AgI solution, the solution was mixed and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded within a minute after sulfide addition. No further signal change was obtained by recording the fluorescence signal at times longer than 1 min after sulfide addition (Fig. S1). Sulfide analysis was performed by determining (F 0-F)/F 0 where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of sulfide, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis using the C-dot/AgI assay
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the C-dot/AgI fluorescence assay was evaluated by analysis of sulfide in a mineral water, a hot spring water (collected from a hot spring in Noor Abad, Fars, Iran) and an industrial wastewater sample (collected from a food products company in Fars, Iran). Each sample was first diluted with BR buffer (pH 12)/NaI solution in such a way that the concentration of BR buffer and NaI in the mixture were 15 mM and 0.15 M, respectively. The mixture was then centrifuged at 3900 rpm for 5 min to remove any precipitate. An appropriate volume of the supernatant was then added to a solution containing C-dot, AgI, BR buffer, and NaI. The final concentrations of C-dot, AgI, BR buffer, and NaI was 30 μg mL−1, 50 μM, 10 mM and 0.1 M, respectively. The samples were analyzed using the standard addition method [28].

Iodometric determination of sulfide was performed according to the procedure described previously [29]. In brief, any particulate matters were first removed from the samples by centrifugation. Sulfide content of the samples was then precipitated as zinc sulfide and the supernatant liquid containing possible interfering substances was removed and replaced with distilled water. Afterwards, an excess amount of iodine was added to the samples in order to oxidize sulfide to sulfur under acidic conditions. The amount of sulfide was then determined by back titrating the unreacted surplus iodine with a standard sodium thiosulfate solution using starch indicator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the citric acid-derived C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots were synthesized via thermal decomposition of anhydrous citric acid as the carbon precursor followed by purification and drying steps [27]. The TEM image revealed that the as-synthesized C-dots were hemispherical particles with diameters below 10 nm (Fig. 1 A). FTIR spectroscopy was used to identify the functional groups in the C-dots. As can be seen in Fig. 1B, the C-dots showed the stretching vibrations of OH (3700–3000 cm−1), stretching vibrations of CO (1870–1540 cm–1), CC stretching modes of unconjugated alkenes (1667–1640 cm−1), stretching vibrations of CO moieties (1300–1000 cm–1), and CH vibrations of alkanes (2963 and 1411 cm−1) and alkene (>3000 and 895 cm−1) [30]. The presence of alcohol, carboxyl, ester, ether, and epoxy functional moieties accounts for the high solubility of the C-dots in water. Zeta potential of the C-dots was determined to be −13 ± 1 mV (Fig. S2), which indicated that the synthesized C-dots were relatively stable [31]. The crystallinity of the C-dots was investigated by XRD analysis (Fig. 1C). The appearance of a broad peak at 2θ = 19.08° (0.46 nm) in the XRD pattern of the citric acid-derived C-dots revealed the existence of amorphous carbon. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the C-dots are shown in Fig. 1D. As can be seen, the C-dots displayed a small absorption shoulder near 350 nm and a fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm when excitated at 355 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots/AgI as a selective fluorescent probe for sulfide analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfide has a strong affinity for Ag+ to form Ag2S, and this interaction has been previously used for the sensitive determination of sulfide [24,25]. Nevertheless, some other species can also interact with Ag+ and as such adversely affect the accuracy of the sulfide analysis. For example, as shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of a solution containing C-dots and Ag+ changed after the addition of not only sulfide but also I−, thiocyanate (SCN−), or glutathione (GSH) to the solution. Consequently, the presence of these species in the sample to be analyzed by the C-dot/Ag+ fluorescent probe leads to inaccurate test results.

One way to improve the selectivity of such an assay is to impede the interaction of Ag+ with species other than sulfide. We achieved this via the employment of I− anion. I− interacts with Ag+ to form AgI, which is among the least soluble silver salts (K sp = 8.52 × 10−17 [26]). In contrast to the case of C-dot/Ag+, no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of the C-dot/AgI was observed with the addition of I−, SCN−, or GSH to the solution (Fig. 2B). Sulfide, on the other hand, interacted with AgI to form Ag2S with much lower solubility (K sp = 6.3 × 10−50 [26]) and caused a decrease in the C-dots fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2B, curve e). Therefore, I− anion enhanced the selectivity of the method via hindering the interaction of Ag+ with the interfering species.

Fig. 3 shows the excitation/emission spectra of the C-dots (30 μg mL−1) and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 200 μM AgI before and after the addition of 50 μM Na2S. As can be seen, the absorption spectrum of AgI (before Na2S addition) overlaps with both the excitation and emission spectra of the C-dots. This spectral overlapping explains the interfering effect of I− observed for the C-dot/Ag+ assay (Fig. 2A, curve b) as it causes a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots when I− is added to the C-dot/Ag+ solution. With the addition of Na2S to the AgI solution, Ag2S forms which shields the excitation light and absorbs the C-dots emitted photons to a larger extent than AgI does. Therefore, the fluorescence of C-dots/AgI in the presence of Na2S is effectively quenched via inner filter effect of the formed Ag2S, which can be exploited for the selective determination of sulfide. A schematic of the C-dot/AgI fluorescence method for the detection of sulfide is presented in Fig. 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and AgI concentration
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the pH at which the highest sensitivity for sulfide analysis can be achieved, we examined the performance of the C-dot/AgI probe over the pH range of 2–12. For each of the tested pH values, we measured the variation in the fluorescence intensity of the C-dot/AgI upon adding 20 μM Na2S to the solution. As is shown in Fig. S3, (F 0-F)/F 0 increases with rising pH from 2 to 12. Since the highest signal change was obtained at pH 12, we chose this pH as the optimum pH for sulfide determination.

The effect of AgI concentration on the performance of the C-dot/AgI fluorescent probe toward sulfide analysis was investigated (Fig. 5 and Fig. S4). Fig. 5A shows the plot of (F 0-F)/F 0 versus sulfide concentration for the C-dot solutions containing 25 and 250 μM AgI. As can be seen from this figure, for the case of 25 μM AgI, the signal change leveled off once the Na2S concentration of ∼12 μM was reached, whereas for 250 μM AgI the signal change continued increasing up to 70 μM Na2S. This can be explained by the limited amount of AgI available for interacting with S2− as the concentration of AgI in the solution was lowered from 250 to 25 μM. On the other hand, the solution containing 25 μM AgI showed large signal changes in response to Na2S concentrations below 10 μM compared to the solution with 250 μM AgI. These results indicate that the use of higher AgI concentrations can provide wider dynamic ranges for sulfide analysis, but at the cost of lower sensitivity. In contrast, lower AgI concentrations offers higher sensitivity but narrower dynamic ranges. Therefore, the dynamic range and sensitivity of the C-dot/AgI probe can be tuned by adjusting the AgI concentration.

To determine the concentration of AgI at which the highest sensitivity for sulfide analysis can be achieved, we prepared four C-dots/AgI solutions with the same concentration of C-dots (30 μg mL−1) but different levels of AgI (25, 50, 100, and 250 μM). For each of these solutions, we measured the decrease in the fluorescence intensity caused by 5 and 10 μM Na2S. As can be seen in Fig. 5B, the signal change upon adding 5 or 10 μM of Na2S increased with lowering AgI concentration from 250 to 50 μM and remained almost unchanged upon further decrease in the AgI concentration. Since the use of 25 and 50 μM AgI led to almost the same sensing sensitivity and by taking into account that 50 μM AgI provides wider dynamic range than 25 μM AgI (see above), we chose 50 μM as the optimum concentration of AgI for obtaining maximum sensing sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sulfide using the C-dot/AgI fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows fluorescence intensity of the C-dot solution containing 50 μM AgI in the presence of different concentrations of Na2S. As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots decreased with elevating sulfide concentration until it leveled off at 12 μM Na2S. The inset shows that (F 0-F)/F 0 was linear over sulfide concentration from 1 to 10 μM with the R2 being equal to 0.9975. The regression equation was y = 0.0096x + 0.0039, where y is (F 0-F)/F 0 and x is the concentration of Na2S in μM. The detection limit (3σ) [28] was estimated to be 0.48 μM, which was superior to many of the recently reported optical methods developed for determination of sulfide (Table 1 ).

To investigate the selectivity of the C-dot/AgI assay, we measured the variations in the C-dots fluorescence intensity in response to a wide range of species other than sulfide, including some environmentally relevant compounds and biothiols. As can be seen from Fig. 7 (open bars), the signal change in response to 7 μM S2− was much larger than that caused by 70 μM of SCN−, PO4 3−, P2O7 4−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O7 2−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, BrO3 −, NO3 −, CH3COO−, NO2 −, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, IO3 − and cysteine (cys). The response to 7 μM S2− was also much larger than that obtained from 14 μM HCOO−, CN−, and GSH. The assay performance toward 7 μM S2− in the presence of each of the foreign species was also studied and is shown in Fig. 7 (hatched bars). The coexistence of these species and sulfide produced signal changes comparable with that observed when only sulfide was present in the solution (the differences in the signal changes were not significant at the probability of 0.05 (or 5%) level [28]). It is therefore concluded that the C-dot/AgI fluorescence assay possesses high selectivity for sulfide determination. Such high selectivity is ascribed to the use of I− to convert Ag+ to AgI with more specificity toward S2−. Indeed, similar measurements without the presence of I− (i.e., C-dot/Ag+ instead of C-dot/AgI) showed the interfering effect of several foreign species including SCN−, SO3 2−, Br−, I−, S2O3 2−, cys, CN−, and GSH on the performance of the C-dot/Ag+ assay (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sulfide in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the C-dot/AgI assay for real sample analysis, we applied it to the determination of sulfide in a mineral water, a hot spring water, and an industrial wastewater sample. The samples were analyzed using the method of standard additions, in which known and different amounts of the analyte were added to the samples [28]. As summarized in Table 2 , good spiked recoveries of 93–108% were obtained for all of the samples. The results indicated no detectable sulfide in the mineral water and the industrial wastewater samples but 1.44 ± 0.09 μM (n = 3) sulfide in the diluted hot spring water sample (corresponding to 0.257 ± 0.016 mM sulfide in the undiluted sample). These results were in good agreement with those obtained by the iodometric method, which detected no sulfide in the mineral water and the industrial wastewater samples and 0.290 ± 0.021 mM (n = 3) sulfide in the undiluted hot spring water sample (the statistical t-test indicated no significant difference at the probability of 0.05 level between the concentrations of sulfide in the hot spring water sample determined by the two methods). To further assess the reliability of the method in determining sulfide in complex matrices, we spiked 0.1 mM sulfide into the sulfide-free industrial wastewater sample and analyzed the obtained sample with the proposed fluorescence and the iodometric methods. The concentration of sulfide in this sample was determined as 0.105 ± 0.010 mM (n = 3) and 0.102 ± 0.011 mM (n = 3) using the fluorescence and the iodometric methods, respectively, and the statistical t-test indicated no evidence of systematic error for any of the methods. These results demonstrate the reliability of the proposed C-dot/AgI fluorescence assay to analyze sulfide in environmental samples.

In conclusion, we showed that the C-dot/AgI fluorescent method developed in this work possesses high selectivity and sensitivity for sulfide analysis. The high selectivity arises from the use of I− with high affinity for Ag+ to form low soluble AgI, which reduces the availability of silver to interact with many other species besides sulfide. Sulfide, on the other hand, interacts with AgI to form Ag2S with much lower solubility than AgI and thereby quenches the C-dots fluorescence intensity. Based on this, as low concentration as 0.48 μM of sulfide can be detected. Despite having high selectivity and sensitivity, the C-dot/AgI fluorescence assay is simple and cost-effective as it does not require any expensive material or functionalization of the C-dots surface. It can therefore be considered as a suitable method for routine analysis of sulfide in environmental samples.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We wish to acknowledge the Iran's National Elites Foundation and Shiraz University Research Council for the support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.150.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Comprehensive Guide to the Hazardous Properties of Chemical Substances
Sulfide as an environmental factor and toxicant: tolerance and adaptations in aquatic organisms
A selective and sensitive sensor for determination of sulfide in aquatic environment
In situ optimization of pH for parts-per-billion electrochemical detection of dissolved hydrogen sulfide using boron doped diamond flow electrodes
Interaction of metallic materials with simulated kraft digester white liquor – towards the electrochemical detection of sulphide
Electrochemical latent redox ratiometric probes for real-time tracking and quantification of endogenous hydrogen sulfide production in living cells
Colorimetric detection of endogenous hydrogen sulfide production in living cells
Polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane polymer-caged silver nanoparticle as a smart colorimetric probe for the detection of hydrogen sulfide
A simple and facile paper-based colorimetric assay for detection of free hydrogen sulfide in prostate cancer cells
Manganese dioxide nanosheets-induced oxidation of dopamine for colorimetric sensing of hydrogen sulfide
Highly sensitive and selective determination of hydrogen sulfide by resonance light scattering technique based on silver nanoparticles
High sensitive luminescence metal-organic framework sensor for hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solution: a trial of novel turn-on mechanism
A dinitro-functionalized Zr(IV)-based metal-organic framework as colorimetric and fluorogenic probe for highly selective detection of hydrogen sulphide
Fast response and highly selective detection of hydrogen sulfide with a ratiometric two-photon fluorescent probe and its application for bioimaging
Detection of sulfide using mercapto tetrazine-protected fluorescent gold nanodots: preparation of paper-based testing kit for on-site monitoring
A terbium(III)-based coordination polymer for time-resolved determination of hydrogen sulfide in human serum via displacement of copper(II)
Cu2+-Mediated turn-on fluorescence assay for sulfide ions using glutathione-protected gold nanoclusters: enhanced sensitivity, good reusability, and cell imaging
Carrot-derived carbon dots modified with polyethyleneimine and nile blue for ratiometric two-photon fluorescence turn-on sensing of sulfide anion in biological fluids
Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications, TrAC
A two-photon "turn-on" fluorescent probe based on carbon nanodots for imaging and selective biosensing of hydrogen sulfide in live cells and tissues
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based ratiometric fluorescent assay for highly sensitive and selective determination of sulfide anions
Metal-ion-mediated fluorescent carbon dots for indirect detection of sulfide ions
Electrochemiluminescence of a nanoAg-carbon nanodot composite and its application to detect sulfide ions
Carbon dot reduced bimetallic nanoparticles: size and surface plasmon resonance tunability for enhanced catalytic applications
Statistics and Chemometrics for Analytical Chemistry
Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater

sb:publisher.sb:name: American Public Health Association, American Water Works Association, and Water Environment Federation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DLS and zeta potential – what they are and what they are not?

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Alireza Abi received his B.Sc. degree in Chemistry and M.Sc. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Shiraz University. He then obtained a Ph.D. in Nanoscience from Aarhus University and did postdoctoral work at Technical University of Denmark (DTU). He is currently a postdoctoral researcher at Shiraz University where his research focuses on sensors and biosensors.
Ghasem Kazemi is a final year B.Sc. student in chemistry at Shiraz University.

Afsaneh Safavi received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry in 1980 from Birmingham University, Birmingham, UK. She is Professor of Analytical Chemistry with Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. Her current research interests include electrochemical and optical sensors and nanosensors, nanochemistry and environmental chemistry. She has published about 300 scientific papers in international journals.
